1: The law of theft: restitution in kind, with penalties scaled to the damage done and the stealth involved. The covenant law's restorative justice principle — paying back more than was taken — reflects the shape of Christ's atoning work: 'where sin increased, grace abounded all the more' (Romans 5:20). The thief must restore; Christ restored what he did not steal (Psalms 69:4, applied typologically to his bearing of others' sin). Restitution defines the moral architecture that Christ's death satisfies.
2: Self-defense against a night-break-in with no guilt if the intruder dies — daylight brings liability. The distinction between night and day in assessing lethal defense reflects a moral principle about proportionality and certainty. The NT equivalent is Paul's distinction in Romans 13:12 between darkness and light as moral categories: 'the night is far gone; the day is at hand.' Moral clarity belongs to the daylight of the gospel; the ambiguities of pre-dawn judgment give way to the clear standards of the kingdom.
3: The thief must restore double what was stolen, selling himself into servitude if unable. The double-restitution principle embeds a surplus of justice: more is paid back than was taken. This is the shape of redemptive economics — Christ gives back more than Adam lost (Romans 5:15: 'the free gift is not like the trespass; for if many died through one man's trespass, much more have the grace of God and the free gift by the grace of that one man Jesus Christ abounded for many'). The law's restitution arithmetic points toward the overflowing grace of the gospel.
4: Restitution for stealing and keeping a living animal: double repayment for a donkey or sheep. The principle of living-thing restitution at double value introduces a proportionality in the law that will later be articulated as the mirror of justice. Christ's atonement satisfies proportional divine justice — not an approximate or discounted settlement but a full and adequate payment that meets the precise demand of the violated law.
5: A grazing animal eating another's field: the best of his own field/vineyard as restitution. The 'best of his own' standard requires not minimal compliance but sacrificial quality in making right what went wrong. The NT parallel is Paul's instruction on generosity: 'whoever sows sparingly will also reap sparingly' (2 Corinthians 9:6). The quality of restitution measures the seriousness of the covenant commitment — a pattern Christ fulfills by giving 'himself for us' as the best possible offering (Hebrews 7:27).
6: Fire spreading from one field to another — accidental but with full liability. The law does not distinguish sharply between intentional and negligent damage in property cases: the harm done creates the obligation, not only the intent. This strict liability principle points toward the comprehensiveness of Christ's bearing of sin's consequences — he bore not only the intentional sins but the full weight of human failure in all its dimensions (Isaiah 53:6: 'the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all').
7: Property placed in trust and stolen: full restitution if the thief is found; divine arbitration if not. The trust-and-betrayal scenario introduces the covenantal dimension of property law — the neighbor is held to the same standard of fidelity as a formal covenant partner. The NT develops the trust-theme in the parable of the talents (Matthew 25:14–30): the master's property entrusted to servants, with strict accountability for its management. Stewardship is covenant faithfulness.
8: When ownership is disputed and the thief not found, both parties come before YHWH (i.e., the judges) and the one found guilty pays double. The judicial process as access to divine adjudication: YHWH is the ultimate judge behind the human court. Jesus claims the same ultimate judicial authority: 'The Father judges no one, but has given all judgment to the Son' (John 5:22). Every human court is a derivative of the divine court to which all disputes ultimately come.
9: Disputes about lost property — ox, donkey, sheep, clothing — all come before the divine adjudicators. The comprehensive list of dispute types ensures no property category escapes the covenant's justice framework. The covenant code is not a selective law for major matters but a total ordering of human community life. Jesus similarly makes total claims on all of life: 'You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind' (Matthew 22:37) — the covenant's total scope is intensified, not reduced.
10: An animal in trust that dies or is injured without anyone seeing: an oath before YHWH settles the matter. The oath-before-YHWH mechanism for unwitnessed events reflects the covenant's understanding that YHWH is always the witness: 'all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account' (Hebrews 4:13). Human witnesses can be absent; YHWH's presence is not. The oath system embeds the knowledge of divine omniscience into the legal order.
11: The oath accepted and no restitution required — but if the animal was stolen, restitution is owed. The oath-mechanism's integrity depends entirely on the reality of divine witness. The NT raises the stakes: Jesus teaches 'let what you say be simply Yes or No; anything more than this comes from evil' (Matthew 5:37). The oath was the legal mechanism for invoking divine witness; Jesus calls his disciples to a level of integrity that makes the oath mechanism unnecessary.
12: If the animal in trust was seized (ganav by predators) and can be shown, no restitution. If killed by a wild animal and its remains brought as evidence, no restitution. The evidential principle — showing what remains — grounds legal decisions in verifiable reality. The NT's faith is similarly evidential: Christ's resurrection is the material evidence on which the gospel rests (1 Corinthians 15:14–15: 'if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile'); the covenant law's demand for evidence of the claim mirrors the gospel's grounding in historical fact.
13: If someone borrows an animal and it is injured or dies in the owner's absence, full restitution. The borrower bears the full risk of the borrowed animal. The NT parallel is stewardship: 'it is required of stewards that they be found trustworthy' (1 Corinthians 4:2). What is borrowed — life, gifts, time, resources — is held under divine ownership and must be accounted for. Christ is the perfectly faithful steward who returned everything the Father gave him fully accounted (John 17:4).
14: If the owner is present when the animal is injured, no restitution — the owner's presence transfers the risk. The owner-present principle establishes that the owner's engagement with his property changes the liability. This maps onto the divine presence principle: when YHWH is present, the people are under his care and responsibility transfers to a different dynamic. Christ's incarnation is the supreme instance — the owner himself is present, bearing the full weight of what happens to his creation.
15: If someone seduces an unbetrothed virgin: he must pay the bride price and marry her; if her father refuses, he still pays the full bride price. The law treats the violated woman's honor as a property matter — a reflection of the law's ancient social context — but its enduring principle is that sexual exploitation creates obligation: the one who takes something that isn't his must pay fully. Christ's treatment of the marginalized and violated (Luke 7:36–50; John 8:1–11) consistently dignifies those the religious system had dismissed.
16: If her father refuses the marriage, the full bride-price equivalent is still paid. The financial obligation stands independent of the social outcome — the law creates accountability even when the relational damage cannot be fully repaired. The covenant logic: what has been wronged must be addressed, even if it cannot be fully undone. This is the structure of atonement — the debt acknowledged, paid, and absorbed even when the harm done cannot simply be reversed.
17: 'You shall not allow a sorceress to live' — the death penalty for occult practice. Alongside idolatry and covenant-breaking, sorcery/witchcraft is a capital offense in the covenant code because it claims access to power outside YHWH's covenant framework: it is a practical denial of YHWH's sovereignty. The NT does not carry the judicial penalty into the new covenant community but maintains the spiritual seriousness: sorcery (pharmakeia) appears in Paul's lists of 'works of the flesh' (Galatians 5:20) and among those excluded from the new Jerusalem (Revelation 22:15).
18: 'Whoever lies with an animal shall be put to death.' The death penalty for bestiality marks the absolute categorical boundary between human and animal — the image-of-God status of the human person sets an inviolable boundary. The NT equivalent is the body-theology of 1 Corinthians 6:15–20: the body of the believer is a 'temple of the Holy Spirit' and cannot be used in ways that violate its nature. The covenant law's boundaries on bodily ethics are intensified, not abolished, in the new covenant.
19: 'Whoever sacrifices to any god other than YHWH shall be devoted to destruction.' The first-commandment seriousness: YHWH's exclusive covenant claim is enforced by the most severe penalty in the judicial code. The NT does not carry the judicial penalty but maintains the spiritual absolute: 'No one can serve two masters' (Matthew 6:24); 'Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers' (2 Corinthians 6:14). The exclusive claim of YHWH on Israel becomes the exclusive claim of Christ on his people — a claim that tolerates no rival at the center.
20: 'You shall not wrong a stranger or oppress him, for you were strangers in the land of Egypt.' The stranger-protection law grounds Israel's ethics in their own history of vulnerability: covenant ethics are motivated by memory. Jesus expands this to the comprehensive love of neighbor (Luke 10:29–37) and fulfills it in his own person — 'the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head' (Luke 9:58). The Lord of the covenant became himself the stranger, so that those who welcome the stranger welcome him (Matthew 25:35).
21: The widow and orphan are placed under special divine protection — the three-fold marginalized category (stranger, widow, orphan) that runs through the prophets and NT. 'Religion that is pure and undefiled before God the Father is this: to visit orphans and widows in their affliction, and to keep oneself unstained from the world' (James 1:27). Jesus's ministry was concentrated among these groups — the marginalized found in him the advocate the covenant had promised them.
22: 'If you afflict them, and they cry out to me, I will surely hear their cry.' YHWH as the direct advocate for those who have no human advocate. The divine responsiveness to the cry of the vulnerable is a covenant commitment: 'he will fulfill the desire of those who fear him; he also will hear their cry and save them' (Psalms 145:19). Jesus identifies himself as the one who hears and answers the cry of those who call on him — 'everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved' (Romans 10:13).
23: If YHWH's anger burns against the oppressor of widows and orphans, their wives will become widows and their children orphans — the covenant consequence mirrors the covenant crime. This lex talionis principle in social justice matters is the structure of divine judgment: what you do to others returns to you (Matthew 7:2: 'the measure you use will be measured back to you'). Christ bears this structure entirely on behalf of sinners — the judgment that should return to us fell on him.
24: Lending to the poor: no interest on loans to the poor among Israel's own people. The no-interest loan to the poor is a covenant act of solidarity — treating the poor person's crisis as a shared community problem rather than an investment opportunity. The NT's theology of generosity follows the same logic: 'lend, expecting nothing in return, and your reward will be great' (Luke 6:35). The covenant code's economic ethics anticipate the kingdom's inversion of normal economic logic.
25: The pledge: if a cloak is taken as security, it must be returned by nightfall — it is the poor person's only covering. The returning-the-cloak law treats the poor person's dignity and physical need as taking precedence over the creditor's legal rights. Jesus radicalizes this: 'if anyone would take your tunic, let him have your cloak as well' (Matthew 5:40). The covenant code's cloak law and Jesus's cloak-teaching are both about radical prioritization of the other person's basic humanity over one's own legal claims.
26: 'If he cries to me, I will hear, for I am compassionate (chanun ani).' The divine compassion is the covenant ground for the social-justice laws: YHWH is compassionate, so his covenant community must be compassionate. The character of YHWH determines the ethics of Israel. The same logic structures the NT ethics: 'Be merciful, even as your Father is merciful' (Luke 6:36); 'be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you' (Ephesians 4:32). Divine character is the ground and model of human conduct.
27: 'You shall not revile God or curse a ruler of your people.' The prohibition against blasphemy and against cursing legitimate authority — both protected by the covenant code. The NT nuances: 'Honor everyone. Love the brotherhood. Fear God. Honor the emperor' (1 Peter 2:17). Paul cites the ruler-curse prohibition when accidentally addressing the high priest disrespectfully (Acts 23:5). The covenant code's protection of legitimate authority becomes the NT's theology of ordered society under God.
28: Firstfruits of harvest and firstborn of sons dedicated to YHWH — the redemption theme embedded in the law. The firstborn-to-YHWH principle was established in the Passover (Exodus 13:2) and developed in the law. The NT picks up this language for Christ: 'the firstborn of all creation' (Colossians 1:15), 'the firstborn from the dead' (Colossians 1:18), 'the firstborn among many brothers' (Romans 8:29). Christ is the true firstfruits offering — the one dedicated entirely to YHWH who by his dedication makes all others acceptable.
29: The firstborn son is to be given to YHWH on the eighth day. The eighth day dedication: circumcision occurred on the eighth day (Leviticus 12:3), Jesus was circumcised on the eighth day (Luke 2:21). The eighth day in Hebrew thought transcends the seven-day week and points toward new creation — the day beyond the Sabbath, the day of resurrection. Jesus's resurrection is on the first day of the week, but this first day is simultaneously the eighth — the day of new creation that the firstborn-on-eighth-day dedication prefigured.
30: 'You shall be holy men to me (anshei qodesh*).' The chapter's closing instruction: the covenant community is a holy community. The holiness demand recurs throughout Leviticus (Exodus 11:44–45; Exodus 19:2; Exodus 20:26) and is explicitly taken up in the NT: 'as he who called you is holy, you also be holy in all your conduct, since it is written: You shall be holy, for I am holy' (1 Peter 1:15–16). Christ is himself the ground of the community's holiness — 'by God's doing you are in Christ Jesus, who became for us wisdom from God, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption' (1 Corinthians 1:30).
31: The torn-meat prohibition: do not eat meat torn by wild animals — give it to the dogs. The holiness principle extends to food — what Israel eats reflects who Israel is. The distinction between clean and unclean food is the bodily enactment of the covenant distinction between holy and common. Peter's vision (Acts 10:9–16) declares all foods clean — but this declaration is simultaneously the declaration that all peoples (not only Israel) are acceptable to YHWH. The food laws' Christological fulfillment is missiological: the abolition of food distinctions opens the table to the nations (Acts 10:28).
